content.problemStatement:
Chef loves to play with arrays by himself. Today, he has an array **A** consisting of **N** distinct integers. He wants to perform the following operation on his array **A**.




Select a pair of adjacent integers and remove the larger one of these two. This decreases the array size by 1. Cost of this operation will be equal to the smaller of them.



Find out minimum sum of costs of operations needed to convert the array into a single element.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N denoting the size of the array A. The next line contains N space-separated integers, where the ith integer denotes the value Ai.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum cost required for the transformation.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Test 1 :  Chef will make only 1 move: pick up both the elements (that is, 3 and 4), remove the larger one (4), incurring a cost equal to the smaller one (3).
Explanation: Chef will make 3 moves: (1,2) -> 1, (1,3) -> 1, (1,4) -> 1. Total cost is 1 + 2 + 3 = 6.
Explanation: Chef will make 4 moves: (5,4) -> 4, (4,3) -> 3, (3,2) -> 2, (2,1) -> 1. Total cost is 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 14.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a dynamic programming approach. We will maintain a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the minimum cost to reduce the subarray from i to j to a single element. We will fill up the dp array in a bottom-up manner. For each subarray, we will try to remove the larger element and add the smaller element to the cost. We will take the minimum cost among all possible ways to reduce the subarray to a single element.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum size of the array A. The reason is that we are using a nested loop to fill up the dp array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) — where N is the maximum size of the array A. The reason is that we are using a 2D array dp of size N x N.
